summary: Jeff Atwood presents his curated reading list for software developers, emphasizing that the best programming books focus on understanding people rather than technology because computers change rapidly but human nature remains constant. The list spans software construction (Code Complete), project management (Mythical Man-Month, Rapid Development), team dynamics (Peopleware), usability and design (Don't Make Me Think, About Face, Design of Everyday Things), and technical craftsmanship (Programming Pearls, Pragmatic Programmer). Atwood notes that the average programmer reads less than one technical book per year, so simply reading these books sets developers apart. The underlying theme is that software development failures stem more from human factors—poor communication, classic mistakes, and misunderstanding users—than from technical limitations.
key_points: The best programming books focus on understanding people, not technology, because computers change but humans don't
Most software projects fail due to repeating classic mistakes rather than encountering new technical challenges
Developers are 'Homo Logicus'—freakish extreme users who mistakenly believe they can make usability decisions for regular users
Reading even one technical book per year puts you ahead of 90% of developers
Usability problems are best solved through quick, dirty testing with real data rather than endless debates
money_quotes: Computers may change, but people don't.
The average programmer reads less than one technical book per year. The very act of reading this book already sets you apart from probably ninety percent of your fellow developers.

The vast majority of software development projects will fail: they will overrun their schedules, produce substandard results, or sometimes not even finish at all. This isn't an argument; it's a statistical fact.
Half of success is not repeating the same mistakes you, or other people, have made.
It doesn't matter how many 'coding superstars' you've got when none of them can talk to each other, or agree on anything.
Developers are freakish, extreme users at best—'Homo Logicus' versus 'Homo Sapiens.'
Designing software is difficult, to be sure, but designing a door is difficult too.
Remember, absolute power corrupts absolutely. But it also rocks absolutely.
key_insight: To build better software, invest in understanding human behavior and avoiding classic mistakes rather than chasing the latest technical trends.
